summary: Gruber investigates a MacRumors report claiming Apple was now asserting that App Store rejection letters are covered by the iPhone SDK NDA. He asks two specific questions: whether this is actually a new policy, and whether the NDA language might just be standard boilerplate from Apple Developer Connection emails. After receiving about two dozen responses from developers, he concludes that the NDA boilerplate has appeared in ADC emails for years across all platforms, and that Apple has not actually changed its policy regarding rejection notice confidentiality.
key_points: MacRumors reported Apple was extending NDA coverage to App Store rejection letters, but the evidence didn't support this conclusion
The 'INFORMATION CONTAINED IN THIS MESSAGE IS UNDER NON-DISCLOSURE' boilerplate has appeared in ADC emails for years, predating the iPhone entirely
Developers who received rejections both before and during the controversy confirmed their notices did not contain new NDA language
One developer who is also an IP attorney received a rejection the same week with no NDA language, undermining the narrative
Gruber distinguishes between genuine Apple policy problems (App Store rejections, SDK NDA) and misattributed outrage based on email signatures
money_quotes: Is it possible, perhaps, that this particular rejection email came from someone in Apple developer relations who includes 'THE INFORMATION CONTAINED IN THIS MESSAGE IS UNDER NON-DISCLOSURE' in their email signature?
Confidentiality guaranteed for anyone who can help answer these questions.

I am an App Store developer, and also happen to be an Intellectual Property Attorney. I received a rejection this week (with the famous 'customer confusion' language, albeit not a 'don't compete with us' type of rejection — this one, while stupid, was easy for me to fix).
It did NOT have the NDA language in the email, and I received it the same day as or the day after the now infamous NDA rejection.

My conclusion is that as fucked up as this entire situation is, both with the App Store rejections for 'duplication of functionality' and NDA frustrations, it does not seem as though Apple has changed its policy regarding whether rejection notices are confidential.
key_insight: The report that Apple was extending NDA coverage to App Store rejection letters appears to be a misinterpretation of longstanding boilerplate email signatures, though the underlying App Store rejection and NDA problems remain real.
